items.q: If a complex ion has a coordination number of 4, what shape does it have?
items.correct: It can be either tetrahedral OR square planar — the shape depends on the metal and ligands (e.g. [Pt(NH3)4]2+ is square planar; [CuCl4]2- is tetrahedral).
items.misconception: A coordination number of 4 always means tetrahedral, because that gives the maximum bond-angle separation.
items.random_wrong: A coordination number of 4 always means square planar because complexes prefer to be flat.
items.why: Students apply VSEPR-style reasoning and default to tetrahedral, missing that d8 metals like Pt(II) form square planar complexes for electronic reasons.

items.q: In 1H NMR, what does the n+1 rule predict?
items.correct: A proton with n equivalent neighbouring protons will appear as a peak split into n+1 sub-peaks.
items.misconception: Splitting tells you how many protons are in the same chemical environment as the one giving the peak.
items.random_wrong: The number of peaks equals the total number of protons in the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse splitting (about NEIGHBOURING protons) with the number of equivalent protons producing the peak (which affects peak area/integration, not splitting).

items.q: What does the M+ peak in a mass spectrum represent?
items.correct: The molecular ion — formed when a single electron is removed from the whole molecule, giving the relative molecular mass.
items.misconception: The M+ peak shows the mass of the largest fragment after the molecule breaks apart.
items.random_wrong: The M+ peak shows the mass of a single hydrogen atom added to the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse the M+ peak (the parent ion at the molecular mass) with fragment peaks; they think the highest mass peak is always a fragment, not the intact ionised molecule.

items.q: Isotopes of an element differ in:
items.why: Correct answer: Neutrons. “Protons” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: ²⁵Mg²⁺ has how many electrons? (Mg, Z=12)
items.why: Correct answer: 10. “12” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Bromine has isotopes ⁷⁹Br (50.7%) and ⁸¹Br (49.3%). Ar to 3 sf is:
items.why: The real figure is 80.0. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
